skill.name: thought-mining
skill.description: Guides users through a structured conversation to capture scattered ideas, identify a core topic, validate facts, improve drafts, and review writing before publication.
skill.summary: Turn unstructured thoughts into clear, reviewed writing through a five-stage conversation workflow.
skill.author: yunshu0909

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-cryptography, and entropy findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks, Chinese prose, and plain YAML description text. No prompt-injection attempt or malicious intent was found. The skill has medium operational risk because it instructs the assistant to create topic-named files and to perform web searches during fact validation.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Filename Creation
medium_findings.description: The workflow tells the assistant to create files named from the user topic. This is useful for writing records, but topic text should be sanitized to avoid unexpected filenames, path separators, or accidental overwrite of existing notes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file creation instructions are explicit and use a topic-derived filename. The risk depends on how the assistant normalizes the topic, so this is a moderate operational concern rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: Markdown Backticks Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported backtick locations are Markdown inline code, code fences, or file tree examples. They do not instruct Ruby or shell command execution and do not include executable command payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown formatting for filenames, templates, and output examples. There is no code interpreter invocation or command string to execute.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alert points to the YAML description line. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, encryption code, hashing code, or password handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain skill metadata written in Chinese. It contains no cryptographic algorithm name or implementation.

low_findings.title: Static False Positive: High Entropy Markdown Content
low_findings.description: The entropy alerts are caused by natural-language Markdown, mainly Chinese text and structured examples. No evidence found of encoded payloads, encrypted content, hidden scripts, or binary data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are readable Markdown with normal workflow instructions. The entropy heuristic likely reacts to UTF-8 Chinese prose and dense punctuation.

low_findings.title: Intentional Web Search Can Disclose Draft Topics
low_findings.description: The validation stage asks the assistant to search the web for official information and market explanations. This is a legitimate writing feature, but sensitive draft topics or private details should not be searched without user approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly requests web search, but the reviewed files do not show hardcoded endpoints or data exfiltration. The risk is contextual and depends on user content sensitivity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Topic-Derived File Path Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses the user topic to form note filenames. Implementations should normalize the topic to a safe basename before creating files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instructions directly define filenames with a topic placeholder. The pattern is visible and should be constrained during execution.

content.user_title: Turn Raw Thoughts Into Publishable Drafts
content.value_statement: Scattered ideas are hard to turn into a focused article. This skill guides a conversation that captures insights, selects a topic, validates facts, improves prose, and reviews the final draft.
actual_capabilities: Guides users through a five-stage writing workflow from idea capture to final review.
Records individual insights while preserving the user's original wording and intent.
Helps identify a core viewpoint from a set of scattered thoughts.
Uses web research to validate technical, product, or industry claims when needed.
Reviews draft logic, wording, memorable lines, factual consistency, and ending strength.
limitations: It does not write a complete article without user participation and source material.
It may need web access to validate facts about current products or industries.
Generated filenames should be sanitized before files are created in a workspace.
It is designed for writing support, not legal, medical, or financial advice.

use_cases.title: Develop an Article Topic
use_cases.description: Turn rough observations into a clear thesis and article direction through guided questioning.

use_cases.title: Structure Product Thinking
use_cases.description: Capture product insights, compare possible angles, and choose the most valuable message for readers.

use_cases.title: Review a Technical Draft
use_cases.description: Check whether a draft matches earlier insights, validates claims, and has a clear final message.

prompt_templates.title: Start Idea Capture
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for opening a writing session.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the thought-mining workflow. I want to write about [topic]. Ask me questions first and record my scattered ideas as insights.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Core Viewpoint
prompt_templates.scenario: Use after several insights have been collected.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the insights we have collected. Help me identify the strongest 3 to 5 points and ask me the one-sentence question to find my core viewpoint.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Claims Before Drafting
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when the topic includes facts, products, or industry claims.
prompt_templates.prompt: Move to the validation stage. Search authoritative sources for the key claims in my topic, then tell me what is accurate, uncertain, or missing.

prompt_templates.title: Perform Final Publication Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for a near-final article.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the final review checklist on this draft. Check topic clarity, logic, factual accuracy, strong lines, ending strength, and give exact revision suggestions.

output_examples.input: I have many notes about AI workflows but no clear article angle.
output: A set of named insights captured from the conversation.
A shortlist of the strongest ideas for the article.
A proposed core viewpoint that the user can confirm or revise.

output_examples.input: Here is my draft. I think the logic may be weak in the middle.
output: Specific wording improvements for unclear sentences.
A final recommendation on whether to publish or revise.

best_practices: Start with free-form thoughts before asking for structure or an outline.
Confirm the core viewpoint before drafting long sections.
Ask before searching the web when the topic includes private or sensitive information.
anti_patterns: Do not ask it to invent insights before you provide real thoughts or notes.
Do not use private names, confidential plans, or unreleased facts in web-search prompts.
Do not accept topic-derived filenames without checking that they are safe and readable.

faq.question: What does this skill help me create?
faq.answer: It helps turn scattered thoughts into a focused article, essay, memo, or structured note.

faq.question: Does it write the whole article for me?
faq.answer: It can help draft and revise, but the workflow depends on your own ideas and feedback.

faq.question: When does it use web search?
faq.answer: It uses web search during validation when the topic includes technical, product, or industry facts.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential writing?
faq.answer: Use caution. Avoid web search for confidential topics and review any generated files before sharing them.

faq.question: What should I prepare before starting?
faq.answer: Bring a topic, rough notes, or a few unfinished thoughts. The skill will ask questions to develop them.
